ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'rice noodle' class (FOODON_03311069); no specific thick rice vermicelli subclass exists. Closest parent match used as thick beehoon is a subtype of rice noodle.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers rice noodles broadly; no specific thick beehoon subtype code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for thick rice vermicelli identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of thick beehoon in Singapore is approximately 200 g of cooked noodles, as commonly served in hawker-centre dishes such as fried beehoon or beehoon soup.

nutrient_source.title: Thick Beehoon — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Thick Beehoon (F05060131). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for thick beehoon (coarse rice vermicelli) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white rice noodles / rice vermicelli, which are reported at GI ~61–72 in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The upper bound of 72 is applied conservatively given the thicker, denser texture of thick beehoon compared to fine rice vermicelli, consistent with the input record's clinical note of GI ~70–80. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 56.0) / 100 = 40.3 (based on 200 g serving × 28.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 56.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 7 mg per 100 g — extremely low sodium content as a plain noodle base
hypertension.guidance: Thick beehoon is inherently very low in sodium (7 mg/100 g) and poses minimal direct hypertension risk; however, patients should monitor total dish sodium from added sauces such as soy sauce or oyster sauce, and request reduced seasoning when ordering at hawker centres.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and 28 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.1 g dietary fibre providing minimal glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thick beehoon is a refined rice-based noodle with a high GI (~72) and low fibre content (1.1 g/100 g), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit portion size, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to attenuate glycaemic response, and consider lower-GI alternatives such as konjac noodles or whole-grain noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.28 g/100 g) and very low sodium (7 mg/100 g); refined carbohydrate with low fibre warrants portion moderation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: As a plain noodle base, thick beehoon is very low in fat and sodium, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk; however, its low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) and high glycaemic load mean that large portions may contribute to elevated triglycerides and insulin resistance, so cardiovascular patients should consume moderate portions and pair with fibre-rich vegetables and unsaturated-fat protein sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with negligible fibre and no anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thick beehoon is a refined starch with minimal fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, polyphenols, or antioxidants); RA patients should use it as a minor meal component paired with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as leafy greens, ginger, or fatty fish, and avoid large portions that contribute to high glycaemic load and pro-inflammatory signalling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thick beehoon (粗米粉; Bihun Tebal) is a type of thick, chewy rice noodle made from rice flour and water, widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Unlike the finer, more delicate thin rice vermicelli, thick beehoon has a substantially larger diameter and a denser, more resilient texture that holds up well to stir-frying, braising, and soup-based preparations. It is a staple ingredient in popular Singaporean hawker dishes such as fried beehoon, beehoon soup, and prawn noodle soup variants. Nutritionally, thick beehoon is predominantly a carbohydrate source (28 g per 100 g cooked), with very low protein (1.57 g), minimal fat (0.28 g), and low dietary fibre (1.1 g). Its sodium content is extremely low at 7 mg per 100 g as a plain noodle, though total dish sodium rises substantially with added sauces. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high GI), making it a food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation — a key consideration for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The low fibre content provides minimal glycaemic buffering. For cardiovascular health, the food is low in fat and sodium as a base ingredient, but its refined carbohydrate nature and high glycaemic load at typical serving sizes (approximately 200 g) warrant portion moderation. Thick beehoon is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal, making it broadly suitable across dietary requirements when consumed as a plain noodle base.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05060131 with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed), applied conservatively at 72 for thick rice noodles. Saturated fat estimated at 0.07 g per 100 g based on USDA FoodData Central rice noodle reference (SR Legacy item 20113) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 20113) as the HPB Foodo source does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is a minor secondary nutrient and does not affect core macro integrity. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for thick beehoon (F05060131), and no supplementary approved source was available to complete these values without risk of misattribution. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for thick beehoon was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. The estimate is based on white rice noodles / rice vermicelli data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 4. The input record incorrectly classified primary_cuisine as 'western' and ethnic_tags as ['western']; this has been corrected to Singaporean cuisine based on the food's established cultural origin. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 56.0 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 40.3 g, classified as very high GL. 6. All core macros (protein_g 1.57, fat_g 0.28, carbohydrate_g 28.0) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required. 7. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
